item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'the key was gone'… wait, wasn't it a letter?

… is too rambly, and the interview feels stiff.
But the core I liked… so…
No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is more like a dream journal than a proper SCP document. The long-winded Description feels like a stream-of-consciousness writing to me, lacking the clinical tone needed in a scientific report. It also makes little sense for the Foundation to simply stop testing just because they're scared of what losing a few notches on the box might entails. The real Foundation would just look for another avenue of research.

I'm on a journey to read every single SCP in a numerical order, and I'm glad this one is over. Won't read it again. Minus one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its a shame this has so few upvotes, i really enjoyed this. it had the right balance of creepy/interesting but not being overly long for my enjoyment, but that is just imho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made a few changes to tie things together a little more thematically, such as the contents of the box and SCP-2382-a.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They aren't going to test more, though, because of the notches. The Foundation doesn't tempt fate when it comes to things counting down to an entirely unknown event. Thinking of the notches, I must say I think it would seem a bit more coherent if they were mentioned at the beginning with the description of the box, instead of as a thing tacked on at the end to seem ominous. Like, the researchers think the notches are just regular damage, but then get surprised after they start disappearing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously, I like this so much. Why isn't it rated higher? I want more testing done, STAT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description of the SCP's effects doesn't feel clinical enough for something implied to only have been gleaned from second-hand reports from non-catatonic test subjects. There are a few decent ideas here, but not enough holding them together, for example:

-Why present potential visitors with a key if they're just going to be forcibly abducted anyway?
-What significance does said key have after disappearing from the subject's grasp?
-Is there any sort of pattern to the after-effects? i.e. is there any relation between the subject's personality or actions while in the gallery and what happens to them before they leave? (If the three effects are tied to the whole "light, life, and tribute" thing, you probably could have expanded on the concept more)

I can see a budding idea here, though; let's run this through SCP-914 and see what happens:

+ show block
– hide block

Otherworldly lich queen "invites" commoners to view her gallery, then steals various "aspects of life" from them with the justification that either the privilege of witnessing her "greatest works" and the consequent appreciation thereof is enough of an equivalent exchange for whatever it is she takes (because she made them herself… and put so much effort into them) with the "level" of loss being proportional to time spent in the gallery and/or how much the subject supposedly enjoyed being there, or alternately she takes something as punishment for not appreciating her clearly superior craftsmanship and artistic vision, because if you cannot use your sight, emotions, or whatever to properly enjoy her obvious artistic genius, then they are wasted on you and you do not deserve to have them.

As for the third aftereffect- who knows? Perhaps if you do actually enjoy her work to a satisfactory degree, she will reward your superior tastes by offering a position by her side forever in her court as one of her UnFaced slaves, meanwhile you awaken in meatspace suddenly aware you are missing something- namely that "spark of humanity" that made you… you- and that said spark has actively abandoned you for the Queen and you futilely rave and plead for it to return to you until you get euthanized or you die (because metaphorically speaking when Engineering gets put in the captain's chair while the captain jumps ship, who's currently maintaining the engines?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's way too much stuff going on here that doesn't tie into anything coherent. I liked the part with the unfinished art pieces, but it reads completely unrelated to anything else in the article. The initial effects are overly convoluted as well. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A good read.

Leaves you wondering a lot of things in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit confusing and long, but otherwise nicely written. You have a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was definitely engaged through the entire thing, and while I don't quite see the connection between the queen and the unfinished art, I did get the "light/life/tribute" thing, and the expungement makes me wonder what the "tribute" is. So, +1 for a good story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty interesting, rambles a bit towards the middle section where there are some awkward-sounding passages (i.e.) "Barring the room referred to as the Throne Room, which is viewed by all who enter SCP-2382-3 (see Interview-2382-1) no single room has been described by two different test subjects."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit confusing, but still interesting. Tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted because I find this interesting and well-written although I have no clue if there is some sort of deeper backstory to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I no-voted this. it's very long and somewhat rambling, but the payoff simply isn't worth it. It's basically a scary nightmare room. The writing itself was pretty good, but the whole article is far too meandering and long-winded to be worth it. I feel like you're trying to do too many effects, but without a sense of a really good story to back it up, like 093, or another work of extreme length.
